title: This race car is made from plant fibers, volcanoes, ... and seawater?
gist: Lola Cars is reviving its 1965 T70 as 16 new low-carbon sports cars using seawater-electrolysis magnesium, a self-developed basalt/flax/sugarcane-resin composite, and a fly-by-wire H-pattern gearbox, cutting the car's cradle-to-gate footprint 54% to 4.6 tons CO2e.

insights.claim: Lola extracts magnesium from seawater via solar-powered electrolysis instead of the carbon-intensive Pidgeon process, producing ingots at massively lower carbon and pollution cost while avoiding harmful shielding gases.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a commercially applied low-carbon magnesium route, relevant to decarbonizing metals supply chains.
insights.quote: Lola extracts the stuff from seawater via electrolysis, powered by solar.

insights.claim: Lola developed its own composite system combining basalt outer layers, flax inner layers acting like Aramid, and a sugarcane-derived PFA resin, which it is already using to build production car parts.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: A bio-based composite in real production shows natural-fiber systems moving from concept to manufacturable parts.
insights.quote: It's a combined fiber system with basalt outer layers, flax inner layers, which is acting kind of like an Aramid, and then a PFA resin system that's derived from sugarcane.

insights.claim: Lola's natural composite is non-structural but outperforms fiberglass in tensile strength and stiffness, with better finish, panel gaps, and dimensional stability than original glass-reinforced plastic bodywork.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Sustainable materials delivering superior quality, not just lower carbon, weakens the performance-vs-green tradeoff narrative.
insights.quote: Lola's natural composite system isn't intended for structural use, unlike Bcomp, but it outperforms fiberglass in tensile strength and stiffness.
insights.locator: Other natural fibers have been incorporated

insights.claim: Lola's lifecycle analysis found the material choices cut the T70S's carbon footprint by 54% versus traditional-material manufacture, yielding a cradle-to-gate footprint of just 4.6 tons CO2e.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: A quantified 54% cradle-to-gate reduction offers a benchmark for low-carbon small-volume vehicle manufacturing.
insights.quote: its efforts have reduced the car's carbon footprint by 54 percent compared to one manufactured with traditional materials

insights.claim: Lola, saved in 2022, is producing 16 new T70 cars in FIA-homologated historic-racing (T70S) and UK road-legal (T70S GT) versions, using the archive revival as a vehicle for materials R&D.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Shows heritage/continuation-car programs can double as sustainable-tech development platforms, not just nostalgia plays.
insights.quote: Lola will have 16 new cars, configured either for historic racing complete with the necessary FIA homologation papers as the T70S, or as a UK road-legal version, the T70S GT.

insights.claim: The author revised his prior view that historic racing offers little tech development, concluding after speaking with Lola that it can drive genuine innovation.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: A contrarian reframe: heritage motorsport as an underappreciated R&D venue.
insights.quote: If you asked me last month, I would likely have said that when it comes to historic racing, it's almost all about the sport and entertainment, with precious little tech development.
insights.locator: To varying degrees, each form of motorsport

insights.claim: The road-going T70S GT uses a modern GM 6.2L V8 (500 hp, 455 lb-ft) to meet emissions and driveability, versus the racing T70S's 5L engine (530 hp, 400 lb-ft), trading peak power for more torque and legality.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Illustrates the emissions-compliance engineering needed to make small-volume heritage cars road-legal.
insights.quote: one of GM's modern 6.2 L V8s that meets emissions and offers modern driveability, rather than the 5 L racing engine
insights.locator: The road-going T70S GT might be a little harder

insights.claim: Lola engineered a fly-by-wire H-pattern shifter with no mechanical link to a modern sequential electronically controlled Hewland gearbox, hiding digital controls behind an analog feel that prevents mis-shifts and enables flat upshifts.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: A 'hide the digital behind the analog' design philosophy blends modern reliability with vintage UX, a template for heritage-product modernization.
insights.quote: So it's an H-pattern selector, but there's no mechanical connection to the gearbox.
insights.locator: So we thought about this, and we came to the conclusion
